content.text: A Variational Autoencoder differs from a plain autoencoder in that both the encoder and decoder are probabilistic: the encoder defines a distribution over latent codes rather than producing a single point, and the decoder defines a distribution over outputs rather than producing a single reconstruction. This probabilistic framing gives the VAE a generative interpretation — the decoder is a proper generative model p_θ(x|z), and samples from the model require only drawing z from the prior and passing it through the decoder.
A VAE defines a joint distribution over observed data x and latent variable z:

The prior p(z) is fixed, typically N(0, I). The conditional likelihood p_θ(x|z) is the probabilistic decoder — a neural network with parameters θ that takes z and returns the parameters of a distribution over x. Generating a new sample from the model is then straightforward:
Sample x ~ p_θ(x|z), e.g. x ~ N(μ_θ(z), σ²I) or x ~ Bernoulli(σ(μ_θ(z)))

The true posterior p(z|x) = p(x|z)p(z)/p(x) is intractable because p(x) = ∫ p(x|z)p(z)dz requires integrating over all z. The encoder q_φ(z|x) is an approximate posterior — a neural network that takes x and outputs the parameters of a distribution over z, typically a diagonal Gaussian:

The encoder parameters φ are trained jointly with the decoder parameters θ. At test time, encoding x produces the mean and variance of the approximate posterior, from which the latent code is sampled via the reparametrisation trick: z = μ_φ(x) + σ_φ(x) ⊙ ε, ε ~ N(0, I).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.distributions import Normal

class ProbabilisticEncoder(nn.Module):
    """
    Encodes x into the parameters of q_φ(z|x) = N(μ_φ(x), diag(σ²_φ(x))).
    Returns a torch.distributions.Normal object for clean downstream use.
    """
    def __init__(self, x_dim, hidden_dim, z_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.backbone     = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(x_dim, hidden_dim), nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim), nn.SiLU(),
        )
        self.mu_head      = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, z_dim)
        self.log_var_head = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, z_dim)

    def forward(self, x):
        h       = self.backbone(x)
        mu      = self.mu_head(h)
        log_var = self.log_var_head(h).clamp(-10, 2)
        sigma   = torch.exp(0.5 * log_var)         # σ from log σ²
        return Normal(mu, sigma)                   # distribution object

    def rsample(self, x):
        """Reparametrised z sample + distribution parameters."""
        dist = self(x)
        z    = dist.rsample()                       # gradient flows through this
        return z, dist.loc, 2 * torch.log(dist.scale)  # z, mu, log_var

# Usage
enc = ProbabilisticEncoder(784, 512, 20)
x   = torch.randn(32, 784)
q   = enc(x)             # Normal distribution
z   = q.rsample()        # differentiable sample
print(q.loc.shape)       # (32, 20) — means
print(q.scale.shape)     # (32, 20) — standard deviations

Making the encoder probabilistic (outputting a distribution rather than a point) serves two purposes. First, it enables the reparametrisation trick so gradients flow through the latent sample during training. Second, the distribution width σ_φ(x) communicates uncertainty: if the encoder is confident about x's latent code, σ is small; if x is ambiguous, σ is large and the sampled z explores more of the space.

Making the decoder probabilistic enables the reconstruction loss to be interpreted as log-likelihood: maximising E_q[log p_θ(x|z)] is equivalent to maximising the average probability the decoder assigns to the true x. This is more principled than minimising an arbitrary distance metric.
Putting It All Together: One Training Step

import math
import torch.nn.functional as F

class VAE(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, x_dim=784, h_dim=512, z_dim=20):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = ProbabilisticEncoder(x_dim, h_dim, z_dim)
        self.decoder = ProbabilisticDecoder(z_dim, h_dim, x_dim, output='bernoulli')

    def forward(self, x):
        # Encode: q_φ(z|x)
        q = self.encoder(x)
        # Sample z via reparametrisation trick
        z = q.rsample()     # z = μ + σ * ε, ε ~ N(0,I)
        # Decode: log p_θ(x|z)
        log_pxz = self.decoder.log_prob(z, x)           # (batch,)
        # KL: closed form KL(q_φ(z|x) || p(z)) for diagonal Gaussian vs N(0,I)
        mu, sigma = q.loc, q.scale
        log_var   = 2 * torch.log(sigma)
        kl        = -0.5 * (1 + log_var - mu.pow(2) - log_var.exp()).sum(dim=1)  # (batch,)
        # ELBO: maximize E_q[log p(x|z)] - KL
        elbo = (log_pxz - kl).mean()
        return -elbo    # minimise negative ELBO

vae   = VAE()
optim = torch.optim.Adam(vae.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
x     = torch.randint(0, 2, (64, 784)).float()

loss = vae(x)
loss.backward()    # stochastic backprop through reparametrised z
optim.step()
print(f"Negative ELBO: {loss.item():.2f}")
returns a tensor with no gradient. The encoder's μ and σ receive no gradient signal. Always use
Treating decoder output as a reconstruction directly:
the decoder outputs distribution parameters (logits or means), not reconstructions. Apply sigmoid/softmax only when computing probabilities for visualisation, not inside the loss.
Not detaching the prior when computing KL:
the prior p(z) = N(0,I) has no parameters; gradients should never flow into it. The closed-form KL formula handles this automatically without needing to construct a distribution object.

Think of the encoder as a witness describing a crime scene: instead of giving one definitive answer, it gives a range of possibilities with associated confidence (a distribution). The decoder is the forensic artist who reconstructs the scene from that description. The VAE trains both to communicate efficiently: the witness learns to give informative descriptions, and the artist learns to produce accurate reconstructions from them.
The VAE encoder q_φ(z|x) is a probabilistic approximate posterior that outputs distribution parameters — not a single latent code.
The VAE decoder p_θ(x|z) is the generative model; its log-probability of the input forms the reconstruction term of the ELBO.
The reparametrisation trick (rsample) is what makes the stochastic encoder differentiable — always use it over sample() during training.
Both components are trained jointly via the ELBO: reconstruction pushes both to preserve information; the KL term regularises the encoder toward the prior.
At generation time only the decoder is needed: sample z ~ N(0,I) and decode. The encoder is only used for inference (encoding real inputs to their latent distributions).
